content: $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{x^4-\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}-1}$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{gathered} \lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{x^4-\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}-1}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{4 x^3-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}}{\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{\frac{8 x^3 \sqrt{x}-1}{2 \sqrt{4}}}{\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}} \\ =7 \end{gathered} $$

content:
Match the following:

In the following, $[\mathrm{x}]$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x .

Column - I Column - II
(a) x | x | x | x | x|x| (i) continuous in (-1, 1)
(b) | x | | x | sqrt(|x|) (ii) differentiable in (-1, 1)
(c) x + [ x ] x + [ x ] x+[x] (iii) strictly increasing in (-1, 1)
(d) | x − 1 | + | x + 1 | | x − 1 | + | x + 1 | |x-1|+|x+1| (iv) not differentiable at, at least one point in (-1, 1)

options.content: $a-i v, b-i i i, c-i, d-i i$
a - ii, b - iv, c - iii, d - i
a - iii, b - ii, c - iv, d - i

explanation:
(a) $x|x|$ $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x^2, & x \geq 0 \\ -x^2 & x<0\end{array}\right.$ differentiable in $(-1,1)$

(b) $\sqrt{|x|}=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\sqrt{x}, & x \geq 0 \\ \sqrt{-x}, & x<0\end{array}\right.$ Not differentiable at $x=0$

(c) $\mathrm{x}+[\mathrm{x}]$ strictly increasing in $(-1,1)$

(d) $|x-1|+|x+1|=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}-x+1-x-1 & x<-1 \\ -x+1+x+1 & -11\end{array}\right.$

Continuous $(-1,1)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc}-2 \mathrm{x} & , \mathrm{x}<-1 \\ 2, & -1<\mathrm{x}<1 \\ 2 \mathrm{x} & , \mathrm{x}>1\end{array}\right.$

content: The function $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}e^x+a x & , x<0 \\ b(x-1)^2 & , x \geq 0\end{array}\right.$ is differentiable at $x=0$. Then
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{c} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{ax} \quad, \quad \mathrm{x}<0 \\ \mathrm{~b}(\mathrm{x}-1)^2 \quad, \quad \mathrm{x} \geq 0 \end{array}\right. \\ & \text { Continuity LHL }=1 \\ & \text { RHL }=\mathrm{b} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=1 \\ & \text { Differentiability LHD }=1+\mathrm{a} \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} \text { RHD } & =-2 \mathrm{~b} \\ 1+\mathrm{a} & =-2 \mathrm{~b} \\ \mathrm{a} & =-3 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: $$ \text { A function } f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}-1}{e^{\frac{1}{x}}+1}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 0, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right. $$

options.content: differentiable at $\mathrm{x}=0$, but not continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$

explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}-1}{e^{\frac{1}{x}}+1} & , \quad x \neq 0 \\ 0 & , \quad x=0 \end{array}\right.\\ &\text { LHL }=-1 \text {, RHL }=1 \text {, Not continuous. } \end{aligned} $$

content: $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2} \cos x-1}{\cot x-1}$ is equal to
explanation:
$\because \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2} \cos x-1}{\cot x-1}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{-\sqrt{2} \sin x}{-\operatorname{cosec}^2 x} \text { [using L-Hospital rule] } \\ & =\frac{-\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{-2}=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos x & x & 1 \\ 2 \sin x & x & 2 x \\ \sin x & x & x\end{array}\right|$. Then, $\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x)}{x^2}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \because f(x) & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \cos x & x & 1 \\ 2 \sin x & x & 2 x \\ \sin x & x & x \end{array}\right| \\ & =\cos x\left(x^2-2 x^2\right)-x(2 x \sin x-2 x \sin x) \\ & =-x^2 \cos x+x \sin x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Now, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x)}{x^2} & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{-x^2 \cos x+x \sin x}{x^2}\right) \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(-\cos x+\frac{\sin x}{x}\right) \\ & =-1+1 \quad\left[\because \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin x}{x}=1\right] \\ & =0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The function $f(x)=|\cos x|$ is

options.content: Everywhere continuous but not differentiable at odd multiples of $\pi / 2$
Neither continuous nor differentiable at $(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in Z$

explanation:
$f(x)=|\cos x|$

Graph of $f(x)=|\cos x|$

From graph we can see that $f(x)$ is not differentiable at points $\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}, \frac{-\pi}{2} \ldots$. . Also, it is everywhere continuous.

i.e. At $x=(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in Z$

$f(x)$ is not differentiable but continuous everywhere.

content: $$\lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{n}{n^2+1^2}+\frac{n}{n^2+2^2}+\frac{n}{n^2+3^2}+\ldots+\frac{1}{5 n}\right)= $$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{n}{n^2+1^2}+\frac{n}{n^2+2^2}+\frac{n}{n^2+3^2}+\ldots+\frac{1}{5 n}\right) \\ & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{n}{n^2+1^2}+\frac{n}{n^2+2^2}+\frac{n}{n^2+3^2}+\ldots+\frac{n}{n^2+(2 n)^2}\right) \\ & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=1}^{2 n} \frac{n}{n^2+r^2} \\ & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=1}^{2 n} \frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{1}{1+\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)^2}\right) \\ & =\int_0^2 \frac{d x}{1+x^2}=\tan ^{-1}(2) \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin (2+x)-\sin (2-x)}{x}=A \cos B$$, then the values of $$A$$ and $$B$$ respectively are
explanation:
We have, $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin (2+x)-\sin (2-x)}{x}=A \cos B$$

$$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos \left(\frac{2+x+2-x}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{2+x-2+x}{2}\right)}{x}$$

$$=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} 2 \cos 2 \frac{\sin x}{x}$$

$$=2 \cos 2 \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin x}{x}=2 \cos 2$$

Thus, $$A=2$$ and $$B=2$$

content: The function $$f(x)=\cot x$$ is discontinuous on every point of the set
explanation:
Given, $$f(x)=\cot x$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad f(x)=\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}$$

We know that $$\sin x=0$$, if $$f(x)$$ is discontinuous

$$\therefore$$ If $$\sin x=0$$

$$\therefore x=n \pi, n \in n \pi$$

So, the given function $$f(x)$$ is discontinuous on the set $$\{x=n \pi, n \in Z\}$$

content:
If $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x^2-1, & 0< x<2 \\ 2 x+3, & 2 \leq x<3\end{array}\right.$$,

the quadratic equation whose roots are $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)$$ and $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x)$$ is
explanation:
$$f(x)= \begin{cases}x^2-1, & 0< x<2 \\ 2 x+3, & 2 \leq x< 3\end{cases}$$

Now, $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} x^2-1=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[(2-h)^2-1\right] =\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[4+h^2-4 h-1\right]=4+0-0-1=3$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { and } \lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x) & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}(2 x+3)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 2(2+h)+3 \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 4+2 h+3=4+0+3=7 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the quadratic equation whose roots are 3 and 7 is given by $$x^2-(3+7) x+(3 \times 7)=0$$ which is $$x^2-10 x+21=0$$

content: $$\lim _\limits{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sqrt{3+y^3}-\sqrt{3}}{y^3}=$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } L=\lim _{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sqrt{3+y^3}-\sqrt{3}}{y^3} \\ & \begin{aligned} L =\lim _{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(\sqrt{3+y^3}-\sqrt{3}\right)}{y^3} \times \frac{\left(\sqrt{3+y^3}+\sqrt{3}\right)}{\left(\sqrt{3+y^3}+\sqrt{3}\right)} \\ =\lim _{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{3+y^3-3}{y^3\left(\sqrt{3+y^3}+\sqrt{3}\right)}=\lim _{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{y^3}{y^3\left(\sqrt{3+y^3}+\sqrt{3}\right)} \\ =\lim _{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+y^3}+\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Consider the following statements

Statement 1 : $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{a x^2+b x+c}{x^2+b x+a}$$ is 1

(where $$a+b+c \neq 0$$).

Statement 2 : $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow -2} \frac{\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{2}}{x+2}$$ is $$\frac{1}{4}$$.
explanation:
Explanation for Statement 1:

Given the limit:

$ \lim\limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{a x^2 + b x + c}{x^2 + b x + a} $

Evaluating this limit at $ x = 1 $ gives:

$ \frac{a \times 1^2 + b \times 1 + c}{1^2 + b \times 1 + a} = \frac{a + b + c}{a + b + c} = 1 $

As long as $ a + b + c \neq 0 $, dividing the numerator and the denominator by the same non-zero quantity results in 1. Therefore, Statement 1 is true.

Explanation for Statement 2:

Consider the limit:

$ \lim\limits_{x \rightarrow -2} \frac{\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{2}}{x + 2} $

This can be rewritten as:

$ \lim\limits_{x \rightarrow -2} \frac{\frac{2+x}{2x}}{x + 2} $

Simplifying further, we have:

$ \lim\limits_{x \rightarrow -2} \frac{1}{2x} $

Substituting $ x = -2 $, we find:

$ \frac{1}{2 \times (-2)} = -\frac{1}{4} $

Thus, the correct limit is $-\frac{1}{4}$, not $\frac{1}{4}$ as stated. Therefore, Statement 2 is false.

content: If $$f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos x & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \cos x & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \cos x\end{array}\right|$$, then $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \pi} f(x)$$ is equal to
explanation:
If $$f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos x & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \cos x & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \cos x\end{array}\right|$$

Expand along $$C_1$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & =\cos x\left(4 \cos ^2 x-3\right) \\ & =4 \cos ^3 x-3 \cos x \\ & =\cos 3 x \\ \therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow \pi} & \cos 3 x=\cos 3 \pi=-1 \end{aligned}$$

content:
At $$x=1$$, the function

$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x^3-1, & 1< x < \infty \\ x-1, & -\infty< x \leq 1 \end{array}\right. \text { is }$$
explanation:
$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x^3-1, & 1< x<\infty \\ x-1, & -\infty< x \leq 1 \end{array}\right.$$

We have to check the continuity at $$x=1$$.

$$\text { RHL } \Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}\left(x^3-1\right)=1-1=0$$

LHL $$ \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (x - 1) = 1 - 1 = 0$$

$$f(1) = 1 - 1 = 0$$

Thus the function is continuous at $$x=1$$.

$$f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} 3 x^2, & 1< x <\infty \\ 1, & -\infty< x \leq 1 \end{array}\right.$$

Now, check the differentiability at $$x=1$$.

LHD at $$x=1 \Rightarrow 1$$

RHD at $$x=1 \Rightarrow 3(1)^2=3$$

As, LHD $$\neq$$ RHD, function is not differentiable.

content:
The right hand and left hand limit of the function are respectively.

$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{e^{1 / x}-1}{e^{1 / x}+1}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 0, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.$$
explanation:
$$\text { We have, } f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{e^{1 / x}-1}{e^{1 / x}+1}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0 \end{array}\right.$$

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{aligned} \text { Right hand limit } & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(0+h) \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(h)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{1 / h}-1}{e^{1 / h}+1} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-e^{-1 / h}}{1+e^{-1 / h}} \\ & =\frac{1-0}{1+0}=1 \quad\left(\because \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} e^{-1 / h}=0\right) \\ \text { Left hand limit } & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(0-h) \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(-h) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-1 / h}-1}{e^{-1 / h}+1} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{e^{1 / h}}-1}{\frac{1}{e^{1 / h}}+1}\right)=\frac{0-1}{0+1}=-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan x}{\sqrt{2 x+4}-2}\right) \text { is equal to }$$
explanation:
We have,

$$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\tan x}{\sqrt{2 x+4}-2}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(\tan x)(\sqrt{2 x+4}+2)}{(2 x+4)-4} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan x(\sqrt{2 x+4}+2)}{2 x} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times(\sqrt{4}+2)=\frac{1}{2}(2+2)=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{1-\cos K x}{x \sin x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ \frac{1}{2}, & \text { if } x=0\end{array}\right.$$ is continuous at $$x=0$$, then the value of $$K$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1-\cos k x}{x \sin x} & , x \neq 0 \\ \frac{1}{2} & , x=0 \end{array}\right.$$

$$f(x)$$ is continuous at $$x=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\cos k x}{x \sin x}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{k}{2} x}{x \sin x}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} 2\left(\frac{\sin \frac{k x}{2}}{\frac{k x}{2}}\right)^2 \times \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\frac{\sin x}{x}} \times \frac{k^2}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 \times \frac{k^2}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{r} \Rightarrow \quad k^2=1 \\ k= \pm 1 \end{array}$$

content: If $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}\frac{\sin 3 x}{e^{2 x}-1} ; & x \neq 0 \\ k-2 ; & x=0\end{array}\right.$$ is continuous at $$x=0$$, then $$k=$$
explanation:
We have, $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}\frac{\sin 3 x}{e^{2 x}-1} ; & x \neq 0 \\ k-2 ; & x=0\end{array}\right.$$ is continuous at $$x=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0) \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 3 x}{e^{2 x}-1}=k-2 \\ & \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\cos 3 x(3)}{e^{2 x}(2)}=k-2(\div \text { form }) \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2}=k-2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k=\frac{3}{2}+2=\frac{7}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Thus any option is not matching

content: $$\sum_\limits{r=1}^n(2 r-1)=x$$ then, $$ \lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1^3}{x^2}+\frac{2^3}{x^2}+\frac{3^3}{x^2}+\ldots+\frac{n^3}{x^2}\right]=$$
explanation:
We have, $$\sum_\limits{r=1}^n(2 r-1)=x$$

$$\Rightarrow x=$$ sum of first $$n$$ odd natural numbers $$\Rightarrow x=n^2$$

Now, $$\lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{1^3+2^3+3^3+\ldots+n^3}{x^2}\right]$$

$$\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right)^2}{\left(n^2\right)^2}\right] \Rightarrow \lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{n^2(n+1)^2}{4 n^4}\right]$$

$$\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{(n+1)^2}{4 \cdot n^2}\right]=\lim _\limits{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{4}\left[\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^2\right]=\frac{1}{4}(1+0)^2=\frac{1}{4}$$

content: Rolle's theorem is not applicable in which one of the following cases?
explanation: Key Idea Rolle's theorem applicable when $$f(x)$$ be a real value function defined in $$[a, b]$$ such that (i) $$f$$ is continuous is $$[a, b]$$ (ii) $$f$$ is differentiable in $$(a, b)$$ (iii) $$f(a)=f(b)$$ then there exists $$c \in(a, b)$$ such that $$f^{\prime}(c)=0$$. We know that $$f(x)=|x|$$ is not differentiable at $$x=0$$.

content: The value of $\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{[x]}{x}$ is :
explanation:
We have,

    $$ \begin{aligned} & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{[x]}{x} \\ & \text { LHL }=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{[x]}{x}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0-h} \frac{[0-h]}{0-h} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-1}{-h}=\infty \\ & \text { RHL }=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{[x]}{x}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0+h} \frac{[0+h]}{h} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{0}{h}=0 \\ & \text { LHL } \neq \text { RHL } \end{aligned} $$

    Hence, limit does not exists.

content:
If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{clc}\frac{\sqrt{1+k x}-\sqrt{1-k x}}{x} & \text { if }-1 \leq x<0 \\ \frac{2 x+1}{x-1} & \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1\end{array}\right.$

is continuous at $x=0$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation:
To ensure the function $ f(x) $ is continuous at $ x = 0 $, the left-hand limit as $ x $ approaches zero from the negative side should equal the function's value at $ x = 0 $.

We have the function defined as:

$ f(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{\sqrt{1+k x} - \sqrt{1-k x}}{x} & \text{if } -1 \leq x < 0 \\ \frac{2x+1}{x-1} & \text{if } 0 \leq x \leq 1 \end{cases} $

Since we want the function to be continuous at $ x = 0 $, we require:

$ \lim_{x \to 0^-} f(x) = f(0) $

For $ x \to 0^- $, we look at:

$ \lim_{x \to 0^-} \frac{\sqrt{1+k x} - \sqrt{1-k x}}{x} $

Using the identity $(a - b)(a + b) = a^2 - b^2$, we can simplify:

$ \frac{\sqrt{1+k x} - \sqrt{1-k x}}{x} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{1+k x} + \sqrt{1-k x}}{\sqrt{1+k x} + \sqrt{1-k x}} = \frac{(1+k x) - (1-k x)}{x(\sqrt{1+k x} + \sqrt{1-k x})} $

This simplifies further to:

$ \frac{2k x}{x(\sqrt{1+k x} + \sqrt{1-k x})} = \frac{2k}{\sqrt{1+k x} + \sqrt{1-k x}} $

As $ x \to 0^- $, the expression simplifies to:

$ \frac{2k}{\sqrt{1+0} + \sqrt{1-0}} = \frac{2k}{2} = k $

The function $ f(x) $ at $ x=0 $ is expressed by the right-side function evaluated as $ x \to 0^+ $:

$ f(0) = \frac{2(0)+1}{0-1} = -1 $

Thus, equating the limits:

$ k = -1 $

Hence, for the function $ f(x) $ to be continuous at $ x=0 $, the value of $ k $ must be $-1$.

content:
If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}\frac{\log _e x}{x-1} & ; x \neq 1 \\ k & ; x=1\end{array}\right.$

is continuous at $x=1$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation:
We have,

    $$ f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{\log _e x}{x-1 ;} & x \neq 1 \\ k ; & x=1\end{cases} $$

    Since this function is continuous at $x=1$

    $$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \lim _{x \rightarrow 1} f(x)=k \\ \Rightarrow & \lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{\log _e \dot{x}}{x-1}=k \end{array} $$

    Apply L'Hospital's rule,

    $$ \begin{aligned} \lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{1}{x} & =k \\ 1 & =k \\ k & =1 \end{aligned} $$

content: $$The\,\,value\,\,of\,\,\mathop {\lim }\limits_{\theta \to 0} {{1 - \cos 4\theta } \over {1 - \cos 6\theta }}\,\,is$$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \lim _{\theta \rightarrow 0} & \frac{1-\cos 4 \theta}{1-\cos 6 \theta} \\ & =\lim _{\theta \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin ^2 2 \theta}{2 \sin ^2 3 \theta} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\lim _{\theta \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^2 2 \theta}{4 \theta^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{\sin ^2 3 \theta}{9 \theta^2}} \cdot \frac{4 \theta^2}{9 \theta^2} \\ & =\lim _{\theta \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{2 \theta}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\frac{\sin 3 \theta}{3 \theta}\right)^2} \cdot \frac{4}{9} \quad\left[\because \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin x}{x}=1\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ =\frac{4}{9} $$

content: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cll}k x^2 & \text { if } & x \leq 2 \\ 3 & \text { if } & x>2\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=2$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$ f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} k x^2, & x \leq 2 \\ 3, & x>2 \end{aligned}\right. $$

Since, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=2$

$$ \begin{array}{lc} \therefore & \text { LHL }(\text { at } x=2)=\text { RHL }(\text { at } x=2) \\ \Rightarrow & \lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} k x^2=\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} 3 \\ \Rightarrow & \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} k(2-h)^2=3 \\ \Rightarrow & k(2-0)^2=3 \\ \Rightarrow & 4 k=3 \\ \Rightarrow & k=\frac{3}{4} \end{array} $$
